resolving_soon.question: Iran x Israel/US conflict ends by April 30, 2026?
Strait of Hormuz traffic returns to normal by end of April?

Trump announces end of military operations against Iran by April 30th?

signals.title: Will GitLab be acquired before 2027?
evidence: No announced acquisition agreement or LOI exists for GitLab as of the evaluation date, and any deal would need to be signed AND closed within ~8 months.
Large tech M&A deals typically take 6-12+ months from announcement to close due to antitrust review (HSR, EU, UK CMA), making a 2026 close extremely tight even if a deal were announced imminently.
GitLab has a dual-class share structure with founder/CEO Sid Sijbrandij retaining significant voting control, creating a structural barrier to hostile or unsolicited acquisitions.

Prior acquisition speculation (e.g., 2023 Reuters report on a potential sale) did not result in a deal, and GitLab has continued executing as an independent public company with no recent strategic review announcement.

Regulatory environment for large tech acquisitions remains stringent; a GitHub-style deal involving a hyperscaler would face heavy antitrust scrutiny that would likely push closing past year-end 2026.
signals.counter_evidence: GitLab's stock has underperformed and AI-driven consolidation in developer tools (e.g., interest from Datadog, Cisco, or private equity) could trigger a surprise deal, though closing by year-end 2026 remains the binding constraint.

signals.title: Will BP be acquired before 2027?
evidence: BP has a market capitalization of roughly $90-100B, making it one of the largest potential M&A targets ever; deals of this scale are exceptionally rare and require 12-18+ months to close.

BP is a UK strategic national asset; any acquisition would face intense regulatory scrutiny from UK government, EU, US, and likely require national security review under the UK National Security and Investment Act.
Reports in early 2025 of Shell considering a BP bid were officially denied by Shell, which made a binding statement under UK Takeover Code rules restricting it from bidding for at least 6 months.
With only ~8 months remaining until the December 31, 2026 deadline, even if a bid were announced today, regulatory approvals and shareholder votes typically take longer than the available window.
No formal takeover bid, activist-driven sale process, or board-sanctioned strategic review for sale has been announced; BP's standalone strategy reset under CEO Murray Auchincloss is being executed.
signals.counter_evidence: Elliott Management's activist stake and persistent press speculation about Shell, ADNOC, or US majors keep nonzero acquisition probability alive, but a closed deal before 2027 remains highly improbable.

signals.title: US x Iran permanent peace deal by April 30, 2026?
evidence: Ceasefire announced Apr 7 was only 2-week deal via Pakistan mediation, not permanent peace treaty.
Historical precedent: US-Iran relations hostile since 1979. No diplomatic ties for 45+ years.
Permanent peace requires: sanctions removal, nuclear deal, recognition - none in active negotiation.
9 days until deadline (Apr 30). Treaty drafting alone takes months.
Iran Supreme Leader has not indicated willingness for permanent normalization with US.
signals.counter_evidence: Ceasefire could be extended and framed as "permanent" if fighting doesn't resume.

signals.title: Iran agrees to surrender enriched uranium stockpile by June 30, 2026?
evidence: Houston Public Media (April 17, 2026): Paxton leads Cornyn 48% to 40% among likely GOP primary runoff voters, with 11% undecided
Emerson College Polling: Paxton consistently polling ahead of Cornyn across multiple surveys since February 2026
FOX 7 Austin: Paxton has higher favorability among Texas Republicans; leads among men, non-college-educated, Latino, and under-65 voters
March 3 primary results: Cornyn 41.9%, Paxton 40.7% — runoff triggered; Paxton closed a narrow gap and now leads in runoff polling

signals.title: JD Vance diplomatic meeting with Iran by April 30?
evidence: Democrats need net +4 Senate seats from 53-47 R deficit to retake control; historically difficult even in wave years
Cook Political Report and Sabato's Crystal Ball rate best D pickup targets as Maine, North Carolina, Ohio — must also defend Georgia (Ossoff) and Michigan (Peters retiring)
In 2018 blue wave, Democrats actually LOST 2 Senate seats despite massive House gains; Senate and House results diverge significantly
Even in strong 2006 wave, Democrats flipped 6 seats — but 2026 map has many R seats in deep-red states unlikely to flip
270toWin/RaceToTheWH Senate forecasts show Senate as significantly more challenging for Democrats than House

signals.title: Will Bitcoin dip to $55,000 in April?
evidence: Bitcoin at ~$69K has held the $65-70K range through 39 days of the Iran war, $112 oil, and Strait of Hormuz blockade. The macro shock is fully priced in. A further 20% crash from here needs a NEW catalyst, not continued war.

Historically, BTC monthly drops exceeding 20% required crypto-specific catalysts: Luna/3AC collapse (-37% June 2022), China mining ban (-35% May 2021), FTX collapse (-16% Nov 2022). Pure geopolitical shocks have not caused 20% monthly BTC crashes.

March 2020 COVID crash (-25%) is the only macro-driven 20%+ BTC monthly drop, and it was a unique once-in-century liquidity crisis with margin calls across all asset classes. The current war does not produce that kind of cross-asset contagion.
BTC's correlation with traditional risk assets has weakened in 2025-2026, with institutional adoption (ETFs, sovereign wealth funds) providing a structural bid that dampens downside.

No crypto-specific risks on the immediate horizon: no major exchange under stress, no DeFi protocol collapse looming, no regulatory crackdown announced. The $55K level would require breaking through multiple support levels ($65K, $60K) with heavy liquidation cascades.
counter_evidence: CoinDesk (April 2): Break below $68K created negative gamma zone — dealers forced to sell as prices fall, potentially pushing below $60K
BeInCrypto (April): Key buyer cohort exiting since January; technical picture threatens 14% correction if $60.5K support fails
Rising leverage + thin liquidity + $112 oil price could trigger liquidation cascades through $65K-$55K range
Bloomberg analyst warned of potential crash to $10K in worst-case scenario citing regulatory headwinds

signals.title: US x Iran ceasefire by May 15?
evidence: Iran REJECTED the 45-day US-backed ceasefire proposal on April 6, demanding instead a permanent end to hostilities, sanctions relief, and guarantees — conditions the US has shown zero willingness to accept.

Both sides are actively ESCALATING, not de-escalating. Iran fired missiles at Saudi Arabia and Bahrain on April 7 (first strikes on Gulf states, widening the war). Trump threatened to bomb all power plants and bridges with an 8PM ET Tuesday deadline.

Resolution requires MUTUAL official ceasefire agreement publicly confirmed by BOTH governments. Even if secret backchannel talks started tomorrow, negotiating, agreeing, and publicly announcing a formal ceasefire in 38 days — while bombs are falling daily — has no modern precedent.

Historical baselines: Serbia bombing campaign lasted 78 days before agreement. Gulf War air campaign was 43 days. Korean War armistice took 2 years. Iran war is only Day 39 with active combat escalation on multiple fronts.

Iran's counter-demands (permanent end to hostilities, sanctions relief, nuclear guarantees) represent maximalist positions incompatible with Trump's demand for unconditional Strait reopening. The negotiation gap is enormous.
counter_evidence: Axios (April 7): Iran talks show "glimmer of progress" — Egyptian mediators say Iran open to 45-day ceasefire with conditions
Trump has political incentive to declare ceasefire victory before midterm season; could accept face-saving deal
Pakistani, Egyptian, and Turkish mediation channels are active — more diplomatic infrastructure than search results suggest
Iran submitted 10-point peace plan (April 7) — rejected by Trump as "not good enough" but shows willingness to negotiate

signals.title: Trump out as President by April 30?
evidence: Trump is Commander-in-Chief of an active war he initiated. Wartime presidents have never been removed mid-conflict — political dynamics strongly favor incumbency during military action.
25th Amendment removal requires VP + majority of Cabinet, all Trump appointees. Impeachment requires 2/3 Senate (67 votes) — Republicans hold majority. Neither mechanism is plausible in 23 days.
No serious impeachment proceedings have been initiated. Congressional war powers debates exist but are far from removal proceedings.
Resignation is implausible — Trump's political identity is defined by fighting. He escalated rather than backed down at every juncture.
Historical precedent: Nixon took 2+ years from Watergate break-in to resignation; Clinton impeachment lasted months. 23 days is structurally impossible for constitutional removal.

signals.title: Will WTI Crude Oil (WTI) hit (HIGH) $200 in April?
evidence: WTI at $115.21 on April 7. Reaching $200 requires a 74% surge in 23 days — would be the largest single-month percentage move in oil history.
Even with Kharg Island strikes beginning and Strait blocked 6+ weeks, supply disruption is partially offset by SPR releases, OPEC+ spare capacity, and demand destruction at current prices.
1979 Iranian Revolution oil shock: prices doubled over ~6 months, not 3 weeks. 1990 Iraq invasion: $17→$41 (140%) took 3 months.
At $200/bbl, massive demand destruction kicks in — airlines ground flights, industrial shutdowns, recession forces begin. Self-limiting price dynamics.
Trump sparing direct strikes on oil export infrastructure until now; even Kharg strikes may target military positions, not tanker loading terminals.

signals.title: Kharg Island no longer under Iranian control by April 15?
evidence: US strikes on Kharg Island began April 7 but bombing ≠ seizure. 'No longer under Iranian control' requires ground/naval occupation or complete abandonment.
Kharg handles 90% of Iran's oil exports — Iran will defend it with everything. IRGC coastal defense (C-802 anti-ship missiles, fast boats) makes amphibious approach extremely costly.
No US ground troops have been committed to territorial seizure in Iran. War strategy is aerial bombardment + blockade, not occupation.
Historical precedent: Coalition air campaign in Gulf War 1991 lasted 38 days before ground invasion; Yugoslavia 78 days of bombing before capitulation. 8 days to seize territory is insufficient.
Even if Kharg facilities are destroyed, Iran retains territorial sovereignty — rubble is still 'under Iranian control' unless physically seized.
signals.counter_evidence: Trump explicitly threatened to destroy Kharg Island if no deal by April 8 deadline, and the 82nd Airborne deployment to the region raises the possibility of a rapid ground seizure attempt.

signals.title: Will WTI Crude Oil (WTI) hit (HIGH) $160 in April?
evidence: WTI at ~$112/bbl. Reaching $160 requires a 43% surge in 23 days — would need catastrophic supply disruption beyond current blockade.
Trump has deliberately avoided striking oil infrastructure (Kharg Island spared despite 90+ military targets hit on March 13).
Even 2008 oil spike ($78→$147) took 6 months; 1990 Gulf War spike was $17→$41 over 3 months. $112→$160 in <1 month is historically extreme.
OPEC+ standing ready to boost output when Strait reopens; strategic petroleum reserves available as buffer.
Iran infrastructure strikes (power plants, bridges) would be devastating but don't directly remove oil from global market.

signals.title: Will WTI Crude Oil (WTI) hit (LOW) $70 in April?
evidence: WTI at ~$112/bbl on April 7. A drop to $70 would require a 37% crash in 23 days — unprecedented without a simultaneous demand collapse.
Strait of Hormuz blocked for 6 weeks (since late Feb); ~20% of world oil transits through it. No reopening imminent.
Even if ceasefire signed today: Strait demining/reopening takes days-to-weeks; physical supply disruption cannot reverse that fast.
OPEC+ agreed to boost output only WHEN Strait reopens (April 5) — supply relief is conditional, not guaranteed.
Trump escalating, not de-escalating: threatening to bomb power plants and bridges by April 7 deadline.

signals.title: US-Iran nuclear deal by April 30?
evidence: Active war ongoing (Day 38); Iran rejected even a temporary 45-day ceasefire on April 6, demanding permanent end to war first.
Nuclear deal requires ceasefire → trust-building → formal negotiations → technical verification — impossible in 23 days.
JCPOA (2015) took 2+ years of intensive diplomacy; Trump withdrew from JCPOA in 2018 and has no diplomatic framework for nuclear talks.
Iran's enriched uranium facilities have been bombed (Natanz, Fordow) — nothing to negotiate over in near term.
Trump's April 7 deadline focuses on Strait of Hormuz reopening, not nuclear issues — nuclear deal is not on the negotiating agenda.
signals.counter_evidence: Trump claimed on April 5 there is a "high chance" of a deal, and regional mediators say negotiations have not collapsed — but his focus is on Hormuz reopening, not a formal nuclear agreement.

US struck 90+ military targets on Kharg (March 13) but deliberately spared oil and gas infrastructure — consistent policy of avoiding oil targets.
No US ground forces inside Iran as of April 6; 82nd Airborne staged in region but no invasion order given.

signals.title: Will WTI Crude Oil (WTI) hit (LOW) $80 in April?
evidence: WTI crude at ~$111-112/bbl as of April 6. Reaching $80 requires a 28% crash within 25 days — historically extreme even during demand shocks.

OPEC+ agreed to boost output WHEN Strait reopens (April 5 announcement) — supply response has multi-week lag even after political resolution.

signals.counter_evidence: EIA forecasts Brent falling below $80/b by Q3 2026 if the Strait reopens; a sudden diplomatic breakthrough or ceasefire could trigger a sharp correction, though not within April.

Preventing Iranian nuclear breakout is a stated primary war objective

signals.title: Will Israel launch a ground operation in Iran by April 30, 2026?

Israel's stated war aims (nuclear program, missile sites) are achievable with air power — no strategic objective requires boots on Iranian soil

82nd Airborne deployment to region could enable joint US-Israeli ground operations
Market question may be interpreted broadly to include commando raids, not just conventional ground invasion
Israel's intelligence on Iranian nuclear sites could drive a targeted ground mission

signals.title: Will UAE strike Iran by April 30?
evidence: UAE has been exclusively a DEFENDER in this conflict — Iran has launched 498 ballistic missiles, 23 cruise missiles, and 2,141 drones at UAE since Feb 28. UAE intercepts them; it does not strike back.
UAE lacks offensive military capability comparable to US/Israel — no stealth bombers, limited strike range, air force optimized for defense and ground support (Yemen), not attacking a nation of 90M

UAE GDP depends on stability and foreign investment — launching an unprovoked strike on Iran would devastate Dubai/Abu Dhabi's position as a business hub and invite catastrophic Iranian retaliation on oil infrastructure
UAE strategy has been to let US/Israel handle offensive operations while maintaining defensive posture — consistent with decades of UAE foreign policy of coalition participation, not solo action
No credible intelligence reporting, military movements, or diplomatic signals indicating UAE offensive planning against Iran — zero mentions in DoD briefings, Reuters, AP, or major OSINT accounts
counter_evidence: Iran killed 1 person and injured dozens in UAE with missile debris (April 3) — could provoke retaliatory response
UAE has invested heavily in military modernization (F-35 interest, THAAD, Patriot) and has combat experience from Yemen
Emirates Global Aluminium facility damaged with 12-month recovery — economic pressure could shift calculus
UAE Crown Prince MBZ has shown willingness to use force (Yemen intervention 2015-2020)
